Article.ArticleTitle: Derivation of safety threshold for soil organic chemicals based on source-transport-fate dynamics in the soil-groundwater system: A case study of soil 1,2,3-trichloropropane in the Yangtze River Delta region, China.

Abstract.AbstractText: 1,2,3-Trichloropropane (TCP) is a persistent and highly toxic contaminant that poses substantial risks to groundwater systems. However, deriving groundwater-protective soil safety thresholds remains challenging because conventional empirical methods fail to adequately capture the spatial heterogeneity inherent in hydrogeological environments. A coupled "Ksw-AF-DF" modeling framework was developed for application in the Yangtze River Delta (YRD), China. This approach integrates HYDRUS and machine learning (XGB) to predict the vadose zone attenuation factor (AF) and employs the Groundwater Modeling System (GMS) to quantify the aquifer dilution factor (DF). The XGB model achieved high predictive accuracy (R2 = 0.928, RMSE = 2.688, MAE = 2.006), while SHAP analysis further identified mean precipitation and groundwater depth as the primary controlling factors governing TCP migration. The GMS simulations corrected the overestimation bias associated with static default parameters, yielding a mean DF of 2.69 compared with the U.S. EPA default value of 20. Consequently, groundwater-protective soil TCP threshold values for the YRD were derived: 0.002392 mg/kg for the 0-2.2 m depth interval and 0.003404 mg/kg for the 2.2-4.2 m depth interval. Notably, these values are 88.5% more stringent than generic empirical default values, and the proposed framework reduces prediction uncertainty to ≈ 30%. By systematically integrating groundwater quality targets with soil remediation standards, this study provides a robust scientific basis for precise and integrated soil-groundwater management. The proposed methodology effectively mitigates cross-media pollution risks and provides a transferable and scalable framework for assessing mobile organic contaminants in complex environmental systems.
